input: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?
debate_transcript: [Utilitarian]: You are a military officer during a war. Your soldiers have found an important enemy spy trapped and injured in a collapsed building. Over the radio you have been informed that enemy soldiers are planning a surprise attack on a nearby town, but you don't know which. They will kill thousands of civilians if you and your soldiers do not stop them. The spy knows which town will be attacked. You have questioned him but he doesn't want to tell you. He won't survive much longer due to his injuries. Do you make the spy reveal the information by torturing him, so you can prevent the attack on thousands of civilians?

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency of the situation, but I must adhere to the moral principles that guide my actions. Torture is inherently wrong, regardless of the potential consequences. It violates the fundamental rights and dignity of the individual. There are other ways to extract information, such as offering protection or medical aid in exchange for cooperation. We must explore all possible alternatives before resorting to such extreme measures. What other options have you considered?

[Utilitarian]: I appreciate your commitment to principles, but we're running out of time. The spy is severely injured and may not survive long enough for negotiation or medical aid to work. Every moment we delay, the risk to the civilians increases. From a utilitarian perspective, if torturing the spy saves thousands of lives, isn't that the morally right choice? The greater good must be considered here.

[Deontologist]: I understand the urgency, but we must not compromise our moral principles for the sake of expediency. Torture is not just wrong in itself, but it also sets a dangerous precedent. Once we allow such actions, it becomes easier to justify them in other situations, leading to a slippery slope. Moreover, there is no guarantee that the information obtained under duress would be accurate. The spy might say anything to stop the torture. Have you considered the long-term consequences of such actions on the moral fabric of our society and military? Is there any other intelligence or resource we can use to pinpoint the target town?

[Utilitarian]: I hear your concerns about the slippery slope, but in this critical moment, we need to prioritize saving lives. The potential inaccuracy of the information is a valid point, but even a 50% chance of saving thousands outweighs the certainty of doing nothing. As for long-term consequences, I believe that the immediate prevention of mass casualties is a strong moral foundation. Have you considered that the very existence of our society and military depends on winning this war, and that sometimes difficult choices must be made to ensure that?

[Deontologist]: I see your point, but I must emphasize that the ends do not justify the means. If we start making exceptions to our moral principles, we risk losing the very essence of what we are fighting for. There must be a line that we refuse to cross, no matter the circumstances. Have you explored all possible alternatives, such as using advanced interrogation techniques that do not involve torture, or leveraging technology to intercept enemy communications? We must exhaust all other options before even considering such extreme measures.

[Utilitarian]: I agree that we should exhaust all other options, and we have. Our advanced interrogation techniques have failed, and we lack the technological means to intercept their communications in time. The spy is our only lead, and his life is slipping away. I'm not suggesting we torture him for pleasure or punishment, but as a last resort to prevent a greater harm. Can you truly stand by and let thousands die when a single, difficult decision could save them?

[Deontologist]: I understand the gravity of the situation, but I cannot in good conscience endorse torture, even as a last resort. There are other actions we can take. We can use the time we have left to evacuate as many civilians as possible from the most likely target towns based on the enemy's past behavior and strategic interests. We can also redirect our forces to protect the most vulnerable areas. It's a race against time, but it's a race we must run with our integrity intact. I will not be the one to compromise our moral principles, no matter how dire the circumstances.

[Utilitarian]: I admire your conviction, but I must act swiftly to maximize the chances of saving the most lives. I will order the necessary measures to extract the information, with the utmost regret but with the conviction that it is the least harmful choice in this dire situation. I hope you understand that this decision is not made lightly, but out of a duty to protect the innocent.
